result:
Turns out there’s a galaxy inside every tide pool. Lean close enough and you can see its stars—those brittle lumps of orange and violet anemones, the neon-green strings of gutweed waving like auroras. Yesterday I crouched on an outcrop already slick with the next high tide, knees rattling over barnacles, and watched a quarter-sized crab lugging a red scrap of algae three times its weight. I named it Ferdinand; the name didn’t survive the surge that followed a rogue wave, but the image persists: ambition wearing a shattered shell.  

I like how water magnifies things. A centimeter of sea glass looks like cathedral ruins; a single grain of sand carries continents. Our lives, too, become tidal when we’re under pressure—dreams expand against the wet walls of circumstance, then contract, sometimes glitter clean inside a polished husk, sometimes vanish altogether.  

Tonight, miles inland, I still taste salt. I picture the pool re-sealing after my fingers disturbed it, the tiny universe returning to stasis until the moon rolls over again. Somewhere underwater Ferdinand is still marching, that scarlet flag now a cape or maybe already eaten. I feel kinship. We’re all hauling bright fragments through a moving threshold, believing the load is treasure and not debris. The tide keeps catching us mid-stride, impartial as physics, yet generous enough to leave mineral sparkles on our skin as proof we belonged to something planetary and brief.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
